steps.title: Quick question first…
steps.question: A heavier object and a lighter object are dropped from the same height in a vacuum. What happens?
options: The heavier object hits the ground first
They hit the ground at the same time
The lighter object hits the ground first
steps.misconceptionFeedback: ⚠️ Common misconception! Many students think heavier = faster falling. In a vacuum, all objects fall with the same gravitational acceleration (g = 9.8 m/s²) regardless of mass. The confusion comes from air resistance in everyday life — not gravity itself.
steps.correctFeedback: ✓ Correct! In a vacuum, mass doesn’t affect free fall. All objects accelerate at g = 9.8 m/s² — Galileo demonstrated this. Everyday confusion comes from air resistance, not gravity.
steps.bestSource: BEST resource: Forces misconception #3 — University of York Science Education Group

steps.title: Newton’s Second Law
steps.bodyHTML: The resultant force on an object equals its mass × acceleration. A bigger force → more acceleration. A bigger mass → less acceleration (for the same force). The force and acceleration always act in the same direction.

relationships: Bigger F → bigger a (if m is constant)
Bigger m → smaller a (if F is constant)

commonErrors: Forgetting to find the RESULTANT force before using F = ma
Using g = 10 instead of 9.8 (always check the mark scheme)

steps.title: See it in action
steps.instruction: Drag the sliders to change force and mass. Watch how acceleration responds in real time.

aqa.questionText: A car of mass 1200 kg accelerates from rest to 18 m/s in 9.0 seconds. Calculate the resultant force acting on the car.

steps.label: Identify the knowns
steps.content:
mass m = 1200 kg
final velocity v = 18 m/s
time t = 9.0 s
initial velocity u = 0 m/s (from rest)

steps.label: Apply F = ma
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct substitution; 1 mark — correct answer

steps.label: State answer with units
steps.content: Always write the unit — examiners award a mark for it.

edexcel.questionText: A lorry has a mass of 8000 kg. The resultant force on the lorry is 12 000 N. Calculate the acceleration of the lorry.

steps.label: Identify the knowns
steps.content:
mass m = 8000 kg
resultant force F = 12 000 N

steps.label: Substitute and calculate
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — substitution; 1 mark — correct answer with unit

ocr-gateway.questionText: A motorcycle has a mass of 250 kg. The engine provides a driving force of 1500 N and the friction force is 500 N. Calculate the acceleration of the motorcycle.

steps.label: Apply a = F / m
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — rearrangement; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

ocr-21c.questionText: A skateboarder of mass 55 kg accelerates at 2.0 m/s². Calculate the resultant force on the skateboarder.

steps.label: Substitute and solve
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — substitution; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

wjec.questionText: A ball of mass 0.40 kg is kicked and accelerates at 25 m/s². Calculate the force applied to the ball.

steps.label: Apply F = ma directly
steps.content: All values given — substitute straight in:
steps.markSchemeNote: 1 mark — correct equation and substitution; 1 mark — correct answer + unit

aqa.questionText: A cyclist and their bike have a combined mass of 80 kg. The cyclist applies a resultant force of 160 N. What is the acceleration of the cyclist?
aqa.markSchemeAward: 1 mark rearrangement · 1 mark substitution · 1 mark correct answer + unit
aqa.commonMistake: Option C is a trap — multiplying instead of dividing gives 12 800.

edexcel.questionText: A skater of mass 60 kg accelerates at 1.5 m/s². Calculate the resultant force on the skater.
edexcel.markSchemeAward: 1 mark correct equation · 1 mark correct answer + unit

ocr-gateway.questionText: An object of mass 12 kg has a resultant force of 36 N acting on it. What is its acceleration?
ocr-gateway.markSchemeAward: 1 mark rearrangement · 1 mark correct answer + unit

ocr-21c.questionText: A car of mass 900 kg accelerates at 3.0 m/s². Calculate the resultant force.
ocr-21c.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark correct answer + unit

wjec.questionText: A runner of mass 70 kg accelerates at 2.5 m/s². Calculate the resultant force.
wjec.markSchemeAward: 1 mark equation · 1 mark correct answer + unit

equation.examTip: Always check you have the RESULTANT force — not just one force. Subtract opposing forces first.

boardNotes.aqa: On AQA formula sheet — must be able to use all three rearrangements.
boardNotes.edexcel: On Edexcel equation sheet. Units must be stated for full marks.
boardNotes.ocr-gateway: NOT on formula sheet — must be memorised for OCR Gateway. ⚠️
boardNotes.ocr-21c: NOT on formula sheet — must be memorised for OCR 21st Century. ⚠️
boardNotes.wjec: Provided in WJEC exam. Know all three rearrangements.
